summary: Teaches how to express 'could' in past (podía) vs future (podría), introduces the pluperfect tense (había + participle) for 'had done', and shows that choosing between imperfect and preterite is context-based rather than a direct translation from English.

teachings.summary: English 'could' has two meanings: past could (I couldn't do it yesterday = podía) and future/hypothetical could (I could do it tomorrow = podría). Past could uses the imperfect of poder.
teachings.rule: Past could = podía (imperfect). Future/hypothetical could = podría (conditional).

examples.spanish: No podía hacerlo porque no tenía el tiempo
examples.english: I couldn't do it because I didn't have the time

teachings.notes: With querer and poder, the imperfect works even when English doesn't use 'was wanting/was able' because wanting and being able are naturally ongoing states.

teachings.summary: Formed with había (imperfect of haber) + past participle. Always uses the imperfect (line) form of haber, never the preterite. Usage mirrors English 'had eaten', 'had gone'.
teachings.rule: Había + -ado/-ido. Always use imperfect of haber for 'had'. Usage is the same as English had + past participle.

examples.spanish: Él quería cenar conmigo pero yo ya había cenado
examples.english: He wanted to dine with me but I had already dined

examples.spanish: Lo buscaba porque yo lo había perdido
examples.english: He was looking for it because I had lost it

teachings.notes: Ya (already) tends to go at the beginning of its clause. Use pronouns (él, yo) when contrasting two people to avoid ambiguity.

teachings.summary: Reviewed habría + participle for 'would have'. The e in habería naturally falls out to form habría.
teachings.notes: The contraction habería -> habría happens so naturally that speakers do it without thinking.
